schema.notes: All user facing text is scrubbed of dashes per Tensor Lab copy rules. Proper dataset and tool names keep their original form. `availability` is the only field the applicant counter footer reads (open or filled). Everything else is descriptive context for the applicant.

fields.project_id: Stable slug, used as the key in the applications sheet and the applicant counter.
fields.availability: open or filled. Drives the footer availability chip on each card.
fields.progress_note: Optional. Work the team has already completed before the fellow arrives.
fields.access_note: Optional. IRB, data access, or de identification status.
fields.mentor_note: Optional. A plain language heads up from the leadership team.

projects.title: LLM Driven Patient Simulation for Enhancing Emergency Department Triage Training
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Medical students, nursing staff, and other emergency room trainees under triage patients at more than twice the acceptable rate. Current ER training tools cannot close this gap because they rely on scripted, non interactive scenarios. Trainees need realistic, conversational practice with real patient presentations before they reach the clinical floor.
projects.technical_approach: Use large language models grounded in de identified MIMIC IV ED and MIETIC case data to generate interactive triage encounters. The trainee takes a history, measures vitals, assigns an ESI level from 1 to 5, and performs critical interventions. The system then compares the trainee's decisions against expert triage labels and real patient outcomes, and it delivers immediate feedback.
projects.deliverable: A deployable web based triage training simulator with LLM driven patient chat, a step by step clinical workflow, and automated performance scoring. Suitable for conference presentation and for submission to a medical education or clinical informatics venue.
projects.data_type: De identified open access data. MIMIC IV ED and MIETIC datasets via PhysioNet. No special access required.
preferred_skills: LLM API integration, for example OpenAI
Web app development, React or equivalent
projects.access_note: Open access datasets. No IRB or credentialing required before the sprint begins.
projects.mentor_note: Great fit for fellows who enjoy building full stack products on top of clinical data.

projects.title: Hormonal and Metabolic Risk Profiling for Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome in Postmenopausal Women
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome and gluteus medius tendinopathy are common and debilitating conditions in postmenopausal women, but clinicians have no reliable way to identify which patients are at highest risk before symptoms become severe. The hormonal, metabolic, and reproductive factors that drive risk are poorly characterized and likely interact in ways that simple regression models cannot capture.
projects.technical_approach: Apply predictive modeling and interpretable ML to the UCSF Information Commons dataset to identify which combination of hormonal, metabolic, reproductive, and demographic factors best predicts who develops these conditions. Build risk stratification profiles for high risk patient subgroups. If needed, validate findings externally using All of Us, UK Biobank, or NHANES.
projects.deliverable: A validated risk stratification model with interpretable feature importance outputs, suitable for publication in a musculoskeletal or women's health journal.
projects.data_type: UCSF Information Commons, de identified and IRB approved. Potential supplemental use of All of Us, UK Biobank, and NHANES, all of which are publicly available or accessible through standard data use agreements.
projects.access_note: IRB is already in place and the dataset is de identified, putting this project in good administrative shape.
projects.mentor_note: The main scoping question before the sprint is which specific hormonal and metabolic variables are available in the Information Commons and whether the outcome, GTPS or gluteus medius tendinopathy diagnosis, can be reliably extracted via ICD code or requires additional phenotyping logic. Resolving that upfront will save significant time during the data preparation phase.

projects.title: Whole Genome Tokenization and Transformer Based Survival Modeling for Multi Ancestry Cardiac Risk Prediction
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine, Institute for Health Computing
projects.clinical_problem: Polygenic risk scores are the current standard for genomic cardiovascular risk prediction. They miss rare variants and perform poorly in non European ancestry populations. This leaves a large share of high risk patients undetected before their first cardiac event.
projects.technical_approach: Build a novel whole genome tokenizer called VDKM using Chaos Game Representation to encode each variant's genomic neighborhood as a compressed coordinate pair. Then train a transformer based survival model to predict 10 year major adverse cardiovascular event risk. Validate the representation in simulation first, then apply it to the All of Us whole genome sequencing cohort, roughly 97,000 participants.
projects.deliverable: A survival prediction model that outperforms standard polygenic risk score baselines across diverse ancestries, plus an open source tokenizer pipeline. Targeted for submission to npj Digital Medicine.
projects.data_type: Controlled access whole genome sequencing data via the All of Us Researcher Workbench. Identifiable. Cloud only access through Google Workbench.
preferred_skills: Genomic file formats, VCF and pysam
projects.access_note: All of Us requires researcher credentialing before data access. Plan for a few weeks of onramp.
projects.progress_note: Preliminary tokenizer design has been drafted. The fellow will start from a working simulation scaffold.
projects.mentor_note: This project predates the Tensor Lab triad structure, so the medical student lead field may not apply in the standard way. Expect deep technical work. Comfort with transformer architectures and survival analysis is important.

projects.title: Orthopedic Outcome Disparities in American Indian and Alaska Native Patients
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: American Indian and Alaska Native patients experience worse orthopedic outcomes than other populations. It is not known which specific factors drive this disparity or whether those factors fully explain the gap. Without that understanding, targeted interventions cannot be designed.
projects.technical_approach: Use supervised machine learning, logistic regression and XGBoost, on the UC Orthopaedic dataset to identify predictors of worse outcomes in American Indian and Alaska Native patients. Then apply SHAP and fairness analysis to determine whether those predictors account for the observed disparity or whether unexplained gaps remain.
projects.deliverable: A disparity analysis with model interpretability outputs and subgroup fairness evaluation. Suitable for submission to a health equity or orthopedic surgery journal.
projects.data_type: UC Orthopaedic dataset. IRB approval is in progress.
projects.access_note: IRB is in progress but not yet approved. Data access is not yet confirmed. The onramp phase needs to move quickly to avoid delaying the summer sprint.
projects.mentor_note: Strong fit for fellows who care about fairness and want to pair ML with rigorous disparity analysis.

projects.title: Causal Inference of Psychiatric Disorders on Hypoglycemia Risk in Type 2 Diabetes
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Patients with common psychiatric disorders, including depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, and schizophrenia, are two to three times more likely to develop type 2 diabetes, yet we do not know how the presence and severity of these conditions affect real world hypoglycemia burden, including frequency, severity, and impaired awareness of hypoglycemia. This gap matters because hypoglycemia is a serious, potentially life threatening complication of diabetes management that is linked to cardiovascular events, cognitive impairment, reduced quality of life, and mortality, but prior work has relied largely on self report and has not rigorously characterized risk across specific psychiatric diagnoses.
projects.technical_approach: Assemble a retrospective cohort of adults with type 2 diabetes from institutional EHR data, requiring at least 6 to 12 months of prior data for confounder assessment and 12 months of follow up for hypoglycemia outcomes. Define exposure by classifying patients into mutually exclusive groups based on EHR documented diagnoses of depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, and schizophrenia, plus a reference group without these diagnoses, and build a pharmacoepidemiologic severity algorithm that integrates psychotropic medication class such as SSRIs, antipsychotics, and mood stabilizers, dose trajectory, number of treatment trials, augmentation strategies, psychotherapy utilization, and mental health related health care utilization as a proxy for psychiatric illness severity. Define outcomes by operationalizing ADA hypoglycemia levels using fingerstick and laboratory glucose values, for example glucose under 70 mg per dL and under 54 mg per dL, ICD codes for hypoglycemia, emergency department visits and hospital admissions with hypoglycemia codes, and administration of rescue therapies including oral glucose, IV dextrose, and glucagon. Construct 12 month outcomes capturing any hypoglycemia, severe hypoglycemia requiring medical assistance or documented level 2 or 3, recurrent hypoglycemia such as three or more events, and exploratory indicators of impaired awareness like severe events in the absence of preceding documented biochemical hypoglycemia or discordance between low glucose and symptom coding. Adjust for baseline covariates including age, sex, race and ethnicity, socioeconomic indicators such as insurance type and neighborhood deprivation proxies where available, HbA1c, duration of diabetes, comorbidity burden, renal function, polypharmacy, prior hypoglycemia, current diabetes treatment regimen including insulin, sulfonylureas, and other glucose lowering medications, glucose monitoring modality such as fingerstick versus continuous glucose monitoring, and concurrent psychotropic medication use. Apply Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation, or TMLE, to estimate the causal effect of baseline mental health status and severity on 12 month hypoglycemia outcomes, using flexible machine learning models such as Super Learner ensembles for both the outcome and exposure mechanisms, yielding marginal risk differences and risk ratios across the four psychiatric diagnoses and severity strata. As a secondary analysis, use causal forests to explore treatment effect heterogeneity by diabetes regimen, for example insulin versus non insulin, sulfonylurea use, GLP 1 and SGLT2 use, and by glucose monitoring modality.
projects.deliverable: A fully adjusted causal inference analysis quantifying the 12 month absolute and relative risk of severe and recurrent hypoglycemia associated with specific psychiatric diagnoses and graded severity, accompanied by subgroup estimates across major diabetes treatment regimens. Includes detailed phenotyping definitions, severity algorithms, and sensitivity analyses covering alternative exposure definitions, alternative hypoglycemia phenotypes, and propensity score truncation. Suitable for submission to a diabetes or clinical informatics journal.
projects.data_type: Institutional EHR data including structured laboratory results, ICD diagnosis and procedure codes, medication ordering and administration records covering psychotropic and glucose lowering agents, glucose monitoring data from fingerstick and, where available, continuous glucose monitoring, and encounter level utilization data. Identifiable clinical data. Requires IRB approval and formal data access provisioning prior to cohort construction.
preferred_skills: Python or R, for EHR scale data wrangling and analysis
Causal inference methods, including TMLE, propensity score based methods, and causal forests or other heterogeneous treatment effect approaches
Experience with epidemiologic study design and large observational health care datasets is preferred
Comfort with automated extraction and phenotyping of EHR data, and with machine learning components inside TMLE such as Super Learner
projects.access_note: IRB approval and data access provisioning required before work begins. Plan for an administrative runway.
projects.mentor_note: Best fit for fellows who want rigorous causal inference practice on real clinical data.

projects.title: LLM Based Cancer Detection Method Classification from Radiology Reports
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Breast cancer detected on screening mammography has different prognostic implications than cancer found on diagnostic workup or MRI. Radiology reports do not reliably structure this information in a way that can be extracted at scale. Automating this classification would enable large scale outcomes research that is currently infeasible.
projects.technical_approach: Run zero shot inference with commercial or open source language models on UCSF breast imaging reports. Then fine tune models to classify the method of cancer detection as screening mammography, diagnostic mammography, ultrasound, or MRI. Compare zero shot and fine tuned performance.
projects.deliverable: A validated LLM classification pipeline for detection method. Suitable for publication in a radiology informatics or breast cancer outcomes journal.
preferred_skills: LLM API usage and fine tuning, Hugging Face or OpenAI
projects.access_note: IRB is in place. De identification requirements for data sharing with the fellow still need to be confirmed before the sprint begins.
projects.mentor_note: IRB is done, which is ahead of most projects. The one remaining step is confirming whether the reports need to be de identified before being shared with the fellow, and if so, what pipeline will handle that. Expect this to be resolved during the onramp.

projects.title: Learning and Predicting Facility Level Cancer Care Quality Using Multi Cancer Data, A Machine Learning Analysis of the National Cancer Database
projects.specialty: Oncology, Health Services Research, Surgical Oncology
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Cancer programs are benchmarked using Commission on Cancer, or CoC, quality measures, but most evaluations focus on single diseases, so it is unknown whether facilities that perform well for one cancer also deliver high quality care across other major cancers. This uncertainty matters because accreditation, referral patterns, and quality improvement efforts often assume a general institutional quality signal that may in fact be largely disease specific.
projects.technical_approach: Use National Cancer Database, NCDB, Participant User File data from CoC accredited programs to construct five organ specific cohorts covering bladder, colon, breast, lung, and kidney cancers, and for each cohort implement one CoC defined process of care quality measure based on standardized numerator and denominator specifications. Build reproducible R pipelines to identify eligible cases and generate patient level adherence indicators, estimate reliability adjusted facility level adherence rates using hierarchical logistic regression with facility random effects, and merge cancer specific facility estimates to quantify cross cancer correlations and identify latent institutional quality phenotypes using traditional correlation measures and exploratory clustering of facilities. Fit multivariable regression and survival models, primarily logistic models for adherence and Cox models with facility effects for overall survival, to evaluate how patient level factors, including age, sex, race and ethnicity, insurance, socioeconomic status, comorbidity, stage, and year of diagnosis, and facility level factors, including program type, urban or rural status, geographic region, and case volume, are associated with adherence, and to estimate the relationship between facility level adherence and overall survival.
projects.deliverable: A multi cancer, facility level analysis of CoC quality measure adherence that describes the distribution and cross cancer consistency of adherence across U.S. cancer programs, identifies patient level and facility level predictors of higher or lower adherence, and quantifies the association between facility level adherence and overall survival. Suitable for submission to a health services research, oncology quality, or surgical oncology journal.
projects.data_type: De identified NCDB Participant User File data, including patient demographics, tumor characteristics, treatments, outcomes, and anonymized facility identifiers. No direct patient identifiers, but use is governed by NCDB data use agreements and institutional review oversight.
preferred_skills: R, for data wrangling and reproducible pipelines
Regression and survival analysis, including hierarchical logistic regression and Cox models with facility effects
Familiarity with quality of care metrics and health services research methods
Optional experience with exploratory machine learning methods such as clustering and simple tree based models
projects.access_note: Requires an NCDB data use agreement and institutional review oversight.
projects.progress_note: In progress. A bladder cancer feasibility pipeline has been completed, demonstrating successful cohort construction, facility level aggregation, and substantial interfacility variation in guideline concordant care. Extension to the remaining cancers and full cross cancer analyses is planned for the upcoming research period.
projects.mentor_note: Well scoped for fellows who like reproducible R pipelines and health services research.

projects.title: 3D CT Segmentation of Paraesophageal Hernias
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Paraesophageal hernia volume predicts emergency presentation and surgical risk. Measuring it requires manual CT segmentation that does not scale to research or clinical use. No automated tool currently exists, and longitudinal volumetric growth has never been studied because the measurement bottleneck makes it impossible.
projects.technical_approach: Train a 3D semantic segmentation model, nnU Net or equivalent via PyTorch and MONAI, on preoperative CT scans from Dr. Carter's surgical cohort to automatically measure hernia sac volume and hiatal defect area. Target an intraclass correlation coefficient of 0.95 against manual ground truth. A larger incidental hernia cohort identified through NLP search of UCSF radiology reports will be used for downstream analysis once the model is validated.
projects.deliverable: A validated 3D segmentation model with automated volumetric outputs, enabling downstream preoperative risk scoring and longitudinal growth rate analysis. Targeted for a surgical or radiology informatics journal.
projects.data_type: Preoperative CT imaging linked to surgical outcomes. Identifiable protected health information.
preferred_skills: Isotropic resampling and cross scanner normalization
nnU Net experience a strong plus
projects.access_note: A de identification pipeline will be applied before data is shared with the fellow. IRB submission is in preparation. Both pieces must be in place before any data reaches the fellow.
projects.mentor_note: This is the most technically demanding project in this cohort. Strongest fit for fellows with hands on 3D medical image segmentation experience, not just general ML skills.

projects.title: Augmented Pseudotime Analysis of Single Cell RNA Sequencing Data to Forecast Trajectories in Traumatic Brain Injury
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Traumatic brain injury unfolds over hours to days, but clinicians currently have no way to predict how a patient's brain cells will change after the initial injury. A tool that forecasts the trajectory of cellular processes from a single tissue snapshot could identify therapeutic targets before damage becomes irreversible.
projects.technical_approach: Use a published single cell RNA sequencing dataset from 34 mice and more than 300,000 cells spanning multiple TBI models, sexes, ages, and timepoints. Apply machine learning techniques to augment pseudotime analysis, which infers cellular trajectories from gene expression snapshots. Test whether those inferred trajectories actually predict future biological states measured at later timepoints. Tooling draws on Seurat and Bioconductor for biological annotation, with machine learning models built on top of the resulting cell by gene matrices.
projects.deliverable: A validated augmented pseudotime algorithm benchmarked against real longitudinal TBI data. Suitable for publication in a computational biology or neuroscience journal and applicable to future datasets generated by the PI's lab.
projects.data_type: Published, fully de identified single cell RNA sequencing data from Jha et al., Neuron 2024.
preferred_skills: Single cell RNA sequencing analysis, Seurat and Bioconductor a plus
Machine learning on high dimensional tabular or sparse matrix data
Familiarity with genomics or transcriptomics a plus
projects.access_note: The lab already has the full dataset in hand. No data access provisioning is needed before the sprint begins.
projects.mentor_note: A strong match for fellows who want to combine single cell biology with machine learning methodology.

projects.title: Interactive Clinical Reasoning Platform for Neurology Education
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Medical trainees struggle to distinguish between neurologic diagnoses that evolve over time. They also struggle to apply quantitative reasoning with sensitivity, specificity, and likelihood ratios in real clinical scenarios. Standard teaching does not provide the interactive, timeline based practice needed to build these skills.
projects.technical_approach: Build a data driven interactive web platform that renders nonlinear neurologic disease trajectories as navigable timelines, with dual patient and clinician views, annotation layers, and progressive disclosure of diagnostic information. Incorporate a data driven backend using the UCSF neurology dataset to ground the cases in real clinical trajectories.
projects.deliverable: A working interactive educational platform with extensible disease modules. Evaluated for its effect on trainee diagnostic accuracy compared to standard teaching.
projects.data_type: UCSF neurology dataset, de identified, IRB approved.
projects.access_note: Data access is confirmed. This project is fully cleared to start.
projects.mentor_note: This project is operationally the most ready in this cohort. The fellow profile is closer to a frontend engineer with data visualization depth than a traditional ML modeler.

projects.title: Incidence, Risk Factors, and Therapeutic Outcomes of Post Cardiac Surgery Vasoplegia
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Vasoplegia, a state of severe low blood pressure with preserved cardiac function, affects up to one in five patients after cardiac surgery on bypass. It is associated with acute kidney injury, prolonged ICU stays, and death. Despite decades of literature, there is no agreed upon definition, no reliable way to predict who will develop it, and no clear evidence on whether targeted therapies like methylene blue actually reduce vasopressor requirements.
projects.technical_approach: To be determined. The AI component for this project has not yet been scoped. The fellow will help shape the modeling approach alongside the PI during the onramp.
projects.deliverable: A retrospective cohort analysis of 480 to 500 adult cardiac surgery patients at UMMC, characterizing institutional vasoplegia incidence, risk factors, and the impact of targeted therapies on 24 hour vasopressor requirements. Suitable for poster presentation and journal submission.
projects.data_type: Institutional EHR and Perioperative Data Warehouse data under Protocol HP 00060609 at UMMC. Identifiable clinical data.
preferred_skills: To be determined pending AI component scoping
projects.access_note: IRB submission is already in progress, which puts this project ahead of most on the administrative timeline.
projects.mentor_note: The AI component is still being scoped. A strong fit for fellows who want early input into how the problem is framed.

projects.title: EEG Based Brain Tumor Classification
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Brain tumors vary significantly in their biological behavior. Current classification depends on pathology obtained during or after surgery. If electrical activity recorded from the brain surface during tumor resection carries information about tumor type, it could offer a new intraoperative or preoperative signal.
projects.technical_approach: Analyze brain surface electrode, EEG, recordings collected during surgical tumor resections at UCSF, combined with spatial distance data, MRI imaging, and post processing outputs. The fellow will evaluate the dataset, identify the most appropriate modeling approach, and implement it. Specific architecture, for example time series classification, graph neural networks, or multimodal fusion, is open and will be determined based on data exploration.
projects.deliverable: A validated model predicting brain tumor type or characteristics from intraoperative EEG and imaging data. Targeted for a neurosurgery or neural engineering journal.
projects.data_type: Intraoperative brain surface electrode recordings with linked MRI and EHR data, collected by a UCSF team.
preferred_skills: Time series or electrophysiology data analysis
Prior EEG or neural signal processing experience a strong plus
projects.access_note: No IRB required. Data access is not yet confirmed.
projects.mentor_note: This project has an unusually open ended scope, which is both an opportunity and a risk. Strongest fit for fellows who are comfortable with exploratory analysis and self directed problem formulation, not just implementing a predefined pipeline.

projects.title: Sex Differences in Adolescent Gray Matter Volume, a Causal Inference Approach Using Twin Neuroimaging Data
projects.institution: University of Maryland School of Medicine, Department of Diagnostic Radiology and Nuclear Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Brain structure differs between sexes during adolescence. Most studies treat sex as a simple covariate rather than isolating its independent effect from overall brain size. Understanding whether sex has a direct effect on gray matter volume, separate from total brain volume, could clarify the biological basis of sex linked differences in neurological and psychiatric conditions that emerge during adolescence.
projects.technical_approach: Apply causal machine learning methods, including propensity score matching and causal forests, to the Queensland Twin Adolescent Brain dataset to estimate the direct effect of sex on gray matter volume while controlling for total brain volume.
projects.deliverable: A causal analysis of sex effects on gray matter volume in adolescent twins, providing a methodological reference for future twin based heritability studies. Targeted for a neuroimaging or computational neuroscience journal.
projects.data_type: Queensland Twin Adolescent Brain dataset. Publicly available multimodal neuroimaging data from twins aged 9 to 14. De identified and open access.
preferred_skills: Causal inference methods, propensity score matching and causal forests
Neuroimaging data familiarity a plus but not required
projects.access_note: No IRB required. Segmentation outputs are already in hand.
projects.progress_note: Baseline segmentation has been completed using AssemblyNet on 416 MP2RAGE scans across 416 adolescents aged 9 to 14. Gray matter, white matter, and total brain volume estimates are ready for each subject.
projects.mentor_note: The AI component is not yet finalized. Strong fit for fellows who enjoy causal inference methodology.

projects.title: Perioperative Risk Prediction in Mandibular Reconstruction
projects.specialty: Surgical Oncology, Head and Neck Surgery
projects.institution: University of California, San Francisco School of Medicine
projects.clinical_problem: Patients undergoing jaw reconstruction for oral cancer face significant risks of surgical complications and death. Surgeons currently lack reliable tools to quantify that risk before the operation. A predictive model could support better preoperative counseling and patient selection.
projects.technical_approach: Apply ensemble machine learning models, including XGBoost, random forest, SVM, KNN, decision trees, MARS, and single layer neural networks, to the National Cancer Database to predict perioperative morbidity and mortality after mandibular reconstruction for oral cavity cancer.
projects.deliverable: A validated risk prediction model benchmarked across multiple algorithm classes. Suitable for publication in a surgical oncology journal.
projects.data_type: National Cancer Database, fully de identified. No IRB required.
projects.access_note: Data access is expected to be straightforward and does not require an IRB.
projects.mentor_note: One of the cleanest data access situations in this cohort. No IRB, public dataset, and a well defined prior benchmark to beat. A fellow can move fast on this one.
